description: Thursday, 18 June 2026 | Primary Production Committee 2026/27 Estimates for Vote Agriculture, Biosecurity, Fisheries and Food Safety Hearing of Evidence: Associate Minister of Agriculture (Animal Welfare, Skills), Minister for Biosecurity, Minister for Food Safety Hon Andrew Hoggard Ministry for Primary Industries Ray Smith, Director-General Gillon Carruthers, DDG Public Affairs Vince Arbuckle, DDG New Zealand Food Safety Karen Adair, DDG Agriculture and Investment Services Stu Anderson, DDG Biosecurity Glenn Webber, DDG Māori Partnerships and Investments Julie Collins, DDG Policy & Trade Neil Cherry, DDG Corporate Services John Roche, Chief Science Advisor
descriptionSummary: 2026/27 Estimates for Vote Agriculture, Biosecurity, Fisheries and Food Safety. Associate Minister of Agriculture (Animal Welfare, Skills), Minister for Biosecurity, Minister for Food Safety. Hon Andrew Hoggard

chunks.text: Oh, look. Are we doing Mandarin and pork health standards, are we? Especially if viewed as a single- No, but we might talk about pork. Oh, yeah. The safety- Save your monk models. Looks pretty ready. Two rachis. Looks pretty ready. Oh, yeah. We'll need food safety to check that one out. Yeah. Why is everybody mad?

Yeah, we've got- Luckily I'm not a doctor. He doesn't even do it because an ice cream is- I am. You're tired, right? My wife's an amazing cook. I've got a baker. Pretty good, isn't it? So the bins are always full of kids and hot food. Right. Welcome. Good afternoon, Minister. Thank you very much for being here today.

Sure. That we'll get you to do some opening remarks, but I was just going to give you a heads up if we could focus on, just by virtue of the panel, we've got animal welfare. Oh, not in relation to your opening remarks, but in terms of questioning, it will be about animal welfare first, and then food safety, and then we're open slather. Okay. So you're out. You're out.

Oh. Oh, no, I didn't mean to do that. Yeah. Okay. Oh, look, thank you very much. I'll just- Two rachis... quickly cover over- Put the two rachis So you've got Ray and everyone else from MPI by the looks of it. Look, for me, going, focus this year, I'll just start with biosecurity.

Obviously, we've got the funding for Wilding Pines, which I'm quite excited by to make sure that gets into work and getting on top of the deferred maintenance and making some headway into some of those seed source areas. Obviously, the other big thing for the last year has been the yellow-legged hornet response, and obviously this year we'll be- Yeah... doing initial monitoring. The fact that we haven't found a single hornet since 2nd of April, I believe, and of all the last nests we uncovered, not a single one of them showed signs

of gines or the structures that would be in place for the next generation of hornets. So that gives a strong signal that, fingers crossed, wood touched, that we got them before they got into that reproduction stage and they would be overwintering. But obviously we can't assume that's the case, and so there'll be monitoring going forward.

One of the other two other items I'll touch on in biosecurity, ongoing work around foot and mouth. We've had one readiness response and exercise in Taranaki that went quite well, and there will be another one coming up shortly, along with a number of other pieces of work in the readiness space and making sure that we have solid plans going forward to be able to respond effectively and efficiently if it were to ever arrive here.

I'm quite amazed some of this hadn't been done previously, but it's been done now, so that's good. And finally, on biosecurity, the other thing I'm probably going to be taking a good hard look at over the next few months is in terms of TB control. There, we've announced the...

Have we announced? Maybe we have a whole TB plan. I don't think so. You just did. I just did? Yeah. Go. Oh, we have. We have. Okay. So there is the new plan that's out there. Good. I was getting a bit worried there. So that's the new operational plan.

But also importantly, I've met a number of times with farmers in Central North Island, really frustrated by the continuing harm that ongoing infection from the wildlife is causing on their farm. So it's going to be really important that we keep a good hard look and make sure that Ospri are doing those control activities at the time they need to do, and they're getting the access they need to.

With regard to food safety, probably the main item between now and the election will obviously be the ACVM bill, which you'll of course have an instrumental role in bringing forward, and I think that's one of the key things we definitely need to deliver is improvements on that system so that we can get these newer and better chemicals and drugs into the country more effectively and cheaply.

We don't need to be reinventing the wheel here in New Zealand when we've got trusted agencies offshore who are doing some of the similar work. Our focus really just needs to be on what is that unique New Zealand perspective that any of these, either be it chemicals or veterinary medicines, might contain in making sure that we're managing risks in that space.

With animal welfare, I think NAWAC's making some good progress in terms of getting codes through. So we've had the deer code signed off. They're at the moment just doing final reviews of the pig code. And hopefully we can put that one to bed.

And also be going out for consultation shortly around some provisions for chickens to do with avian influenza preparedness. The dairy code is waiting for sign-off. Sheep and beef code isn't too far away, and the rodeo code will shortly be going out for draft consultation. So, some good progress in terms of getting through these codes.

They had been slow going for a while there, but making some good progress now. With that, I'll open up for questions. Right. Fantastic. Go, Rachel. Thank you. Thank you, Chair. Thank you. So animal welfare? Yes, animal welfare. That's right. And so, I'm keen to just talk a little bit about dog welfare.

Yep. First of all, though, I just want to say well done on deciding not to proceed with the reinstatement of live cattle export by sea. So it was good to see that the government made that decision. So I just wanted to acknowledge that. But on dogs- Excuse me. Can I just interrupt for one second? Yeah.

I'm very sorry. Is everyone happy if the, ACT guy takes photographs? Yes. Right. Thank you. There we go. Good to go. Yeah, okay. Sorry to interrupt. We won't Photoshop him. Yeah. You can talk to the speaker. Yeah. So on dogs, so obviously one of the things you've previously done was- Tethering... yeah, it was the ending of chained- Yeah... dogs. So I just kind of wanted to do a little bit of a look back and a look forward.

Yeah. One of the things that happened then was the containment of dogs in terms of life containment, so maybe being in a crate- Yeah... in a confined space, was separated out. Do you have an intention of looking at the domestic code of welfare, so for dogs, and then looking to reinstate that work specifically around containment as well?

So with regard to the code, I've sent a clear signal. I want to know where to prioritize getting through the production codes first, and then obviously the dog code would be next. In terms of containment, recently had a meeting with SPCA, they've already done some work on a propo- what their proposed sort of regulation might be in this space.

They have consulted with others, and they've sent it through. I just actually read it this morning. And so I'll be seeking advice from NPI on, do they have any suggestions otherwise on that? So yeah, I am looking at that. It seemed very much the same way in terms of the tethering, where you were looking at what are the signs of prolonged tethering.

Not trying to guess how long someone had left an animal on a chain, but more that here are the indicators that this animal has been left in distress for too long. But yeah, before we move forward, obviously want to get some expert advice on are these the right indicators, are there ones that are missing?

But that's something we can look to take forward in that regard. Yeah. So I've just got some stuff just generally around dog welfare. So, a related issue, which I guess sits in a separate portfolio- Yeah... is the reform of the Dog Control Act. Yeah. So my question is, how much are you integrating that work with the Dog Control Act? Because obviously there is some relationship there.

There's some elements to it in terms of with the dog code, it's quite tenuous, the links, though. Hmm. There are links but they're not very strong. I think the two could stand up separately. I don't think they need to happen precisely at the same time. So there's stuff that I can progress now- Yeah... and will look to do it.

Agree with that. I guess it's just more if you're seeing overlap between things that are in the Dog Control Act that might, and then stuff in the code and whether you're working- Look, I think there's elements where you look at the challenges where you've got roaming dogs. And that can- That's my main thing... often be from where you've got people who are unlicensed, well, just breeding, doing the puppy mill stuff.

So there'd be elements in the dog code that could well encourage behaviors that might avoid some of that. They're not necessarily the end all and be all. Yeah. If you follow me. But you see my point, right? That, yeah, if you do one thing in one part of the law, you want to try and get it right in the other.

Yeah. And just one final question is actually to do with puppy farms. Yeah. And so whether that's something that you're looking at as part of the dog code reform or as part of just wider animal welfare concerns around, particularly the breeding dogs who are often kept in a cage and just breed and breed and breed, and whether you're looking at any of the welfare components around that.

Look, I think there's an element obviously of the confinement issue that feeds into that. They were going to send us some stuff on the puppy mills as well. Yeah. I haven't seen that one yet but, yeah, that was also something RSPCA raised with us. Sorry, just got one final quick sup, which is just about the ability for the likes of the SPCA, but also councils and then even dog rescue agencies to actually get in and rescue a dog because often that's the challenge, right? Is that people know there's a dog, or might know there's a dog in distress.

The SPCA might, but it might not meet their threshold to actually go on site. The animal welfare officers at councils might not actually have the right to go in and remove a dog. Is that something that you're considering across all of this reform, the ability for people to actually get in, remove a dog that's in clear distress, so it- I'd have to pass this one off to Fiona in terms of I have a feeling we didn't quite have those powers. No.

Yeah. We've done a bit of work under the Emergency Act. Mm-hmm. Even done some work around being able to have our animal welfare officers remove dogs in that context. Right. But, I don't think we've got those powers under the Animal Welfare Act, and we haven't considered them at this point. It would require another wide piece of work, I think.

Thank you. Right. We've got a couple of sups. One from Brooke first, and then Steve. Yeah. I'll leave my sup and go to the primary if I can afterwards. Well, you're in a queue for that. Thank you. We can do that. I'm also kind of interested in the dog area. So thank you for actually making some moves on the dog tethering area, because I know that's been particularly popular in my electorate of Tāmaki, where we have actually had some dog tethering issues, especially- In Glen Innes.

Yeah. And I know it's something my community has cared about for a number of years, and it's great to see a minister actually taking action. In my other role as Minister of Internal Affairs, I've given out a fairly substantial grant to the SPCA- Mm... in another area, which was to do with the feral dogs. Now, that's for urban and rural.

And the aim is to get a couple of hundred dogs off the street and get them completely desexed so that they don't end up having more and more puppies and- Yeah... continuing the problem. Yeah. I do know that it's a little bit of a issue in some of our rural areas with those dogs, you know, attacking farm animals. Is that a statistic that you guys keep hold of and work with SPCA on? Because- Uh... I've just kind of left it to SPCA to do the good work that they do, but do they actually share statistics with you on that basis?

I don't know if we've got stats on that. I dare say in rural areas, farmers will often solve that problem themselves. Mm-hmm. Put it bluntly. Yeah. Might not be a question for you, but maybe someone else in that crowd. It's a problem. Yeah. A lot of dog attacks. It is a problem, yeah. Yeah. Very much so. And I think, I've historically seen it where we used to farm, up against the Terera Ranges. Lots of people wanted to go in there for pig hunting.

They'd often lose their pig dogs. Mm. And that became a bit of a problem- Interesting... for, back many, many years ago, when my grandmother had sheep. Yeah. Okay. No, it's good. I'll query the SPCA on what sort of statistics they get. It's most likely, it's a good question, and we'll follow that. It's most likely with the dog control authorities and the council.

So I suspect- Oh... yeah, it might more likely cross over. It's with the dogs. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. Okay. But no, good work on the dog tethering. Yep. And keep going on it. Cheers. Yeah. Sorry, did you have a question? Oh, no. It's all right, because I was going to ask about wild dogs. Okay, so we've got a question from Rachel. Oh, no, you've had your question. Sorry, Joseph. Oh, thank you. Water plans.

Yeah. It was great- Oh, hang on. Animal welfare, isn't it? Animal welfare. Yes. And then storm. Yeah, we're still on animal- Oh, no, sorry. I wasn't. Mine was a new one. Yep. Don't worry. No, we're doing animal welfare till 1:35. Oh, we are? Sorry. Okay, go for it. 2:35. 2:35. 2:35, sorry. That's another thing. Shall I go, Cec? Yes, you're next. Thank you. This is your favorite part of scrutiny, Minister.

Oh, you got some photos. Oh, he's got pictures. I got some photos. Would have never guessed. So, this is this month. No. Yep. And, this is just the start of the season, as you well know. Oh, yeah. And, we've already got in Southland cows in mud. This just bec- seems to be like a, a very familiar and normalized sight, that we have intensive strip grazing and winter grazing, and the cows spending a large part of the day in mud, and there's not appropriate places for lying.

We've often issues with water. When there's a big deluge of rain, which hasn't yet happened, the situation gets markedly worse. You currently have in the estimates a budget of around 26 million, I think, for, managing and enforcement around- Yep... animal welfare.

It just seems insufficient to deal with it because, one, either, and I put this to you, you've accepted this as a perennial reality of the way farming is gonna be done here and nothing's gonna be done about it. Or two, if this isn't acceptable, there's insufficient action being taken to actually address it.

So in terms of the last season, there were, someone can correct me from behind, but I think around 211- Stephen... or, oh, there you are. 276. 276, uh, inspections. Now, in terms of... I mean, the point I'd make, and yes, you don't wanna see animals in deep mud, but the reality is none of that is deep mud.

Every single cow you're showing there is in condition score five or above. Okay. And so, if you were to say, "Okay, we're not gonna use winter crops anymore," and for that part of the country, that means what do they do instead? What else? Yep. Do they properly feed those animals? Do they stop them for, shift them for hours and hours to somewhere else?

The key things here are, we wanna make sure the animals are well fed, that they've got water, that they can access those things, that they're not, have been forced into deep mud where they can't lie down and they're uncomfortable, and that we don't have that sediment going into waterways. Those are the key things that we wanna be managing.

To say we don't want winter grazing, it is an effective tool that farmers, particularly in the south, need for being able to feed their animals properly over winter. And the risk of not doing it, means that you've got underfed animals and poorly conditioned animals, which is no good for the animal.

But isn't it a matter of stocking intensity? And you're saying that what you see there is satisfactory from your perspective. But we're in June now. Yep. And the worst of the winter rains, or spring rains actually, are gonna come as we get closer to the end of the year. So a situation that already looks like that- They're not gonna be, I mean, these animals are there for June and July.

That's when they're there for, to that period, and then they'll be into, they'll be getting into July, they'll be getting close to calving, so they'll be going off into the springer mob, which will be on pasture. And they'll be starting to grow grass and then moving into the normal rotation on the farm. But during those months, particularly down south, they just do not grow feed.

So- The risk is if you force everyone to go and put their cows only on grass all over the farm, then we get to spring when the pregnancy happens and the peak demand comes on them, and they've got no feed, and their body score condition collapses, they struggle to produce, they have all sorts of other animal welfare issues.

To me, winter grazing is a useful, and particularly down south, a very important tool to help manage the welfare of the animals. Now obviously, we don't want to see them being in situations where they can't lie down, or they can't properly access water or feed, or that there's environmental harm being caused by it.

So we're not going to see calves born into mud this season? No. There will probably be a calf born into mud. I had a cow calf two days ago. That was not on the plans. They don't fill out a memo as to when they're going to calve- ... and which paddock they're going to calve, at what time they're going to calve, and whether they need assistance or not.

Correct. That is the reality of farming. Sure. An animal will arrive early. The main thing is that the farmer should know that from this time onwards, they need to be off. You'll always get a cow that will calve early. And from my past experience, I've had situations where cows are on grass, and 20 of them have all decided in one night in a storm, "Let's go and all sit in this corner, have all our calves together, and then run around in a circle bellowing at the calves." And I get up in the morning and suddenly discover, oh my God, I've got a big muddy patch with a whole bunch

of calves. Unintended consequences, it happens. You don't want it to happen. But these things will happen. So you're okay with this scene? Those cows are in great condition. I think you're probably looking in the foreground at the wettest part of the paddock.

It looks like further back there is a lot more drier. And again, you can see none of them are over their hoof height in terms of the soil. Some here maybe. Well, again, what does the rest of the paddock look like? Can you share it with the rest of the committee, Steve?

Have you had a massive overnight rain? Yeah. People can take a picture of one small section of a paddock and not get the full context. Sure. Okay. Are you okay with that? Can I just get another question? No, that's not great because you're wasting feed in a boggy patch.

Probably a better place to put the baleage in the ring feeder. Are you okay with the animal welfare issue? The animals- I asked you about animal welfare... condition score five or better. Okay. In terms of adequate lying off. Steve Well, I don't know what the rest of the paddock looks like.

There's possibly a great part of the rest of the paddock. If you were to give- You know the cows are stuck in a strip. They don't have access to the paddock. Yeah, but I don't know what the rest of the strip looks like. That looks like a hollow there, Steve. Now, the rest of the paddock, we've gotten one small little smidgen of what probably a 200, so we're looking at perhaps 20 square meters of a 200 hectare farm.

Okay. Thank you very much. We're going to move on. And so I can't make a judgment on the entire farm based on that. I do have a supplementary. Yeah. It's very directly related to this. I'm the member of Parliament for Southland. We have an activist called Matt Coffee who often takes photographs like this, and I don't know if that's the one, to supply to the Green Party and to put on national news. He takes very selective photographs that try to spread his particular narrative.

It's not helpful and it's not reflective, and if the minister could confirm what breaks are in terms of feeding cows, confirm that we have 100 days of the year roughly in Southland where grass does not grow. Yeah. And also confirm that if the cows were on this grass trying to eat from places where the grass is not growing, there would be much more significant impact on- Yeah... the environment and potentially on the cows than a managed- Is there a question?

Yes. Managed winter grazing program. Yeah. Look, I think you could very well have even worse environmental outcomes if you try to just go grass only in Southland over that time. Particularly, you could end up damaging vast chunks of your farm, compacting the soil, which will lead to higher nitrate leaching, and prevent future grass growth.

There's been a great amount of work done by industry groups along with the MPI in terms of winter grazing plans to help people understand the best ways to do this, to do it smartly, to maximize the welfare of the animals, and to protect the environment. And that's why I encourage all farmers to-- And where it doesn't happen, we have prosecuted.

Where people have done it poorly, we haven't held back from prosecuting them. Thank you. Thanks, Minister. Does anyone else have any questions about- Well, if I could just- Animal welfare... just on the question that I asked then. Yeah. I appreciate I'm hearing a lot of them, but- We'll try and stick to the budget, shall we? Because that's what we're talking about.

We're talking about different- Just the last one, just on break feeding. If you could just explain what that is, just for the context of that photograph Well, it's basically you offer a fresh strip of feed to the animals, generally first thing in the morning, and usually that's intended to be, you work it out based on how much feed is there.

So that's the animal's daily ration. You'll probably provide baleage or some other form of supplementary feed to ensure that- Just in the context of those photographs there? Yeah, that is strip grazing. Yes. Yeah. It's strip grazing. But they- That's what I'm saying. They've got nowhere else to go. They're in the strip. They did have feed. Well, you talked about strip doing feed across. Yeah. Hey, look, Peter, I'm going to move this on now because I think we've- Yeah. But I've got another question on animal welfare

... drifted off topic. Does it relate to the budget? Yes, it does. Mr Abel. Thank you very much. Great. So Minister, you spoke about the codes of welfare that you're developing. Yeah. Is there any intention- Well, Nayworks... that Nayworks' developing Is there any intention to develop a code around fish, given we recognize them as sentient in our animal welfare acts?

I believe that is possibly on the thinking, but it's currently-- obviously we've got a priority around production animals at the moment, and then the existing companion animals. I know there has been some thought given to it in NAWAC, but as yet, I don't believe I would know. I haven't received request for that.

And I've got a follow-up on- A supplement. Yeah. On the fish one? Yes, on the fish one. Yep. Yep. So, specifically farmed fish as opposed to all fish. Yeah. So farmed fish, I understand Agriculture New Zealand might be working with MPI on MPI adopting, because there's a voluntary code held by Agriculture New Zealand- Yes... that could be quite a straightforward solution for MPI to adopt that for use as a code.

Uh- See nods from officials I know we're working with them on that in terms of adoption, as you all like to remind me, in Parliament last year, there is a process that has to go through with NAWAC. Yeah. I've got another code question. Where's things at on the pig code in terms of NAWAC?

So obviously after the decisions that were taken, there were a few matters that we wanted them to review in terms of, okay, these decisions have been made in that context. Just getting some further advice on that, and a range of stakeholders have also been involved in that discussion.

Okay. Yep. Good. And I believe that finished up recently. Yeah. So the meetings have finished. The consultation is closing, I think Friday. Yeah. And report back, will we see anything anytime soon or? Maybe. NAWAC is due to provide a response on the basis of all of that consultation to the minister, and then the minister goes through his regular process around issuing a code.

Yeah. Before the end of term, or? We'll see when we get the advice back and how much time's left and, yeah. Okay. Thank you. Okay. Any more questions on animal- Welfare. Animal welfare? I'm good. Animal health, I count. Okay, so we can-- You wanted to move on to biosecurity?

Well, Rachel Brooking isn't here. But she's due out in two minutes, is she? Yes. We could do other stuff. Well, does anyone else have a question on um- I've got biosecurity. I've got questions on food safety. Do you want to do food safety? Okay. Go food safety.
That was what we planned on doing next. Yeah.

I'm trying to understand what is going on in the imported blueberry space. Right. So I've had a conversation with some, I think it'd be fair to say, distressed local producers who are concerned about a liberalization of the rules around importation of fresh blueberries.

Yep. And there are real concerns about the multitude of potential hitchhikers on those fruit, such as fruit flies of various different varieties and other species. And one of the food safety related things, this is in the biosecurity space as well. But the food safety related thing is, apparently, MPI is advised that it's acceptable that where a blueberry has come in contact with the ground, that it can be wiped clean and sold as fresh fruit in New Zealand, but that that is not a practice that occurs here in local production. Can you speak to that at all?

Look, and I'll just jump over to the biosecurity side quickly. Sorry. That was my fault for- Yeah... going on. So there is an import health standard being developed in terms of the fruit fly stuff. The controls around it will be identical to other imported plant or fruits and stuff, where we have controls around fruit fly, is what I've been told.

Obviously, I haven't seen the final standard yet. It's all still being worked on. In terms of the... I think our Mayor de Vince or someone else know more about that part. We should find out the specific, but I- I remember it in the discussion, but I can't remember exactly what the correct answer was... I think what you could feel confident in is we are not going to allow the importation of a food product into New Zealand that's not safe. I mean, we are probably amongst the most rigorous set of import health standards worldwide.

Mm. Right? So, that's not going to happen. One of the issues here is we export blueberries- Mm... and we've sought trade relation. We've sought through other countries the same thing, where they have wanted to hold our blueberries out, and we've managed to get trade and market access because the blueberry sector in New Zealand have wanted us to get that for them, and we've got that.

In the same way that the countries that we trade with Want- Access... to be able to access and have their products come to New Zealand, who've been through a very rigorous process on this. It takes a long time. Mm. And I'd be very confident that there's no way we would allow a product into New Zealand that was gonna make people sick or that was going to bring in a pest or disease. It's an incredibly rigorous process, open to challenge.

I think they've challenged the process. It's all in a formal structure- Mm... to resolve any of those issues. But I think the fundamental issue is New Zealand trade, we export 93% of our food. Yes. That's how we make our money. Yeah. We have to be open to receiving some as well. Yeah.

Yeah. And I think there's always tension. There's quite a bit of tension in the financials in that part of the sector. Just on the food safety side of it, so we already know blueberries are a high-risk- That's right... product for food safety- Yeah... in terms of frozen blueberries. Yeah. How is that exacerbated by- Mm... them being fresh, in terms of the risk of, um- It's more the conditions in which they're picked and the health of the pickers.

That's right. Yeah. The hepatitis A risk is from pickers- Yes... for countries where hepatitis A is endemic. Yes. It's not endemic in New Zealand. So, it doesn't really matter whether it's fresh or frozen, it's the contact with the picker. So that's an ongoing issue, and that issue is not increased by the fresh nature of the product-... but it is an issue that comes with imported- Absolutely... fruit.

Yes. It's less likely in, far less likely in New Zealand and more likely in some countries. Yeah. So how do we manage that problem practically then? Yeah. The controls that we have in place are good agricultural practice. Yeah. So, ensuring that New Zealand sources from locations where they've got good hygiene practices. Some countries actually vaccinate their workers- Right... to ensure they lower that risk.

But since we had that issue with frozen berries, we've introduced new controls at the border, which requires, let's look. Requires, um- I really want to laugh, it's fine that way... importers to be assured that they're dealing with- Oh, it's not going to go... with a producer that's got a good control, and it's basically about hygiene with the workers really.

Make sure that the risk of hepatitis transmission is minimal. Okay. Okay, we've got a question from Dana. Thank you. I just wonder if we could just take a step back on the food safety thing, just briefly, so that I could understand a bit better what the main priorities are and the outcomes that you're looking for in the next 12 to 24 months in the wider food safety portfolio, and how that success will be measured- Uh... for the country.

For me personally, it's about that import, sorry not the import health standard, the ACVM changes. And I think we'll be working out targets with the team in terms of how many new chemicals, et cetera, and the timeframe it takes to get them over the line, reducing the queue.

Mm-hmm. Those are sort of the measures will be put in place there. I think within our system, we've got a range of metrics that I don't quite know off the top of my head around Campylobacter and a whole range of other diseases that we want to make sure that these things are kept at a low level.

Mm. And I guess the other point, I've had in place what we call the Food Act Improvement Plan, which is around reducing some of the perhaps compliance barriers for some of the less riskier operations and smaller operations- Mm... to enable those businesses.

We had an announcement nearly a year ago around the home-based cake makers. Removing rules and regulations from them. We've had export extensions have passed. So when another country says, "This is the formulation that we want on a product," then those exporters don't have-- And if it's different to the New Zealand formulation, they don't have to go through this long, complicated process to be allowed to do it to that formulation for offshore.

It's just like, if that country's happy with it, here you go. So, just carrying on with some of that work of making sure the system is a bit more agile. I sort of feel like there's a lot of rules and regulations that are in place for if you're Fonterra-sized.

Mm. But if you're a bit smaller, it's quite a bit heavier or- Mm. And that prevents some of the smallers eventually ending up as Fonterras, because there's a bit of a barrier to growth there. So we're just trying to make things a bit more risk proportionate. Yeah. Okay. Thank you. Thank you very much. Oh, one other, I was just going to ask you about biosecurity.

There's been a bit of noise. Oh, Rachel, yeah. Oh, I'm sorry, you're here now. You want to talk about food safety? Yeah. Well, I just thought you were getting ready, so I was just going to- Oh, sorry. What are we on? We've jumped- Food safety. We didn't have a structured agenda. Yeah.
